project_overview.project_development_objective: Improve access to safe drinking water and irrigation water services in selected rural areas
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1.2 million people in rural areas of 16 drought-affected provinces, with priority to vulnerable populations including women and girls

geographic_scope.coverage_type: Regional - 16 drought-affected provinces covering approximately 120 districts
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - Afghanistan is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. The project operates in a context of political instability, control by the Interim Taliban Administration (ITA), ongoing drought crisis, and deep economic and social crises. The project is designed to operate outside direct ITA control through community-based mechanisms.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Provision of Emergency Water Supply in Identified Rural Areas
key_components.description: Construction and rehabilitation of small and medium-scale climate-resilient water supply systems including drilling of new wells/boreholes (10,224 new water points), rehabilitation of water systems and networks (15,226 rehabilitated systems), provision of water supply to schools and health facilities, implementation of public awareness campaigns on WASH practices, and provision of chlorine tablets and hygiene supplies. Ownership of assets transferred to Community Development Councils (CDCs) for operation and maintenance.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Improved Surface Water Irrigation Using Solar Technologies in Selected Rural Areas
key_components.description: Piloting Energy-as-a-Service (EaaS) business model by contracting Private Energy Service Companies (PESCs) to construct, install, and operate solar-powered irrigation pumps and associated equipment. Project provides 80% of capital costs; PESCs provide 20% in-kind contribution. Includes feasibility assessments, equipment procurement, PESC screening and selection, installation and operation of solar systems, and community consultations. Assets owned by PESCs upon satisfactory performance.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Technical Training and Public Awareness Campaigns
key_components.description: Development and delivery of technical training modules for water-related entities with focus on women's participation. Training targets technical experts, management personnel, and community-level platforms. Awareness campaigns on efficient water use, sanitation, and renewable energy benefits for irrigation. Coordination with Health Emergency Response Project to train female community health workers on WASH.

Support for project management and oversight costs for AKF-USA (US$6 million) and UNOPS (US$4 million), including technical support, training, monitoring, evaluation, coordination, and establishment of Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM).

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Joint implementation by Aga Khan Foundation USA (AKF-USA) for Components 1 and 3, and United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS) for Component 2. AKF-USA will establish a Project Implementation Unit (PIU) in Kabul and engage AKF-Afghanistan and three NGOs as Project Implementing Entities (PIEs). UNOPS will establish a PIU and engage Private Energy Service Companies (PESCs) as subgrantees.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Steering committee composed of World Bank, AKF, UNOPS, UNICEF, USAID, and other water sector practitioners to coordinate and avoid duplication. Community Development Councils (CDCs) serve as central decision-making bodies for project identification and implementation. Mirabs (community-elected water masters) and irrigation associations manage water resources at community level. ARTF Monitoring Agent provides independent verification and monitoring.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: AKF-USA follows World Bank Procurement Regulations for Components 1 and 3, with support from national procurement experts. UNOPS follows Alternative Procurement Arrangements (APA) under UN procedures for Component 2. Central procurement by UNOPS for main goods (solar panels, pumps, pipes). World Bank provides Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) for Components 1 and 3. Forced labor bidder declarations required for solar procurement.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: AKF-USA responsible for M&E of Components 1 and 3; UNOPS responsible for Component 2 M&E. Data collection by provincial offices and PIEs, verified by PIUs and cross-checked through field sampling. ARTF Monitoring Agent provides independent verification. Quarterly progress reports and semi-annual Implementation Status and Results (ISR) reports submitted to World Bank. Project MIS tracks expenditures and results. Beneficiary satisfaction surveys planned during implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk of political interference in project activities by the Interim Taliban Administration (ITA), control of geographical areas by different political or armed factions, potential restrictions on women's participation and movement, and discriminatory bans affecting UN and INGO operations. Risk of inadequate targeting of vulnerable people due to political interference.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Working with politically neutral implementing partners (AKF and UNOPS) with long-term presence and trust. Remote project supervision arrangements. Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) ensuring CDCs are not prohibited from operating and women's involvement is not prohibited. Communities given three weeks to meet ECA or project moves to other areas. Clear mechanisms to identify security threats and communicate threat level changes. Business continuity plans for implementation if situation changes.

identified_risks.risk_description: High macroeconomic risks including potential discontinuation or reduction in aid due to donor fatigue, stoppage of US cash shipments affecting exchange rate stability, banking sector instability due to payment resolution challenges and transition to Islamic banking, and impact of climate events such as droughts. Country faces major economic crisis with 46% of population acutely food-insecure.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project designed as emergency response with two-year implementation period to deliver immediate results. Flexible project design allowing for scaling and adaptation. Coordination with other donor-funded projects to leverage complementary support. Focus on building community resilience to droughts through water and irrigation interventions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Sector priorities have shifted and may be revised again depending on country situation, adding to sectoral volatility. Weak and fragmented water sector institutions with slow implementation of new Water Regulatory Law and National Water Sector Strategy.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stronger coordination across interventions, partners, CDCs and communities to strengthen rural household resilience to droughts. Component 3 activities strengthen sector knowledge and public awareness on hygiene and water management at local and grassroots level to influence local decision-makers. Leverage traditional community-led institutions (CDCs, Mirabs, irrigation associations) which are robust.

identified_risks.risk_description: Multi-sector emergency project covering both drinking water and irrigation implemented by two groups of implementing agencies. Complexity due to multiple sub-sectors and geographical coverage. Risk of technical design becoming overly complex.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Six-month inception period to identify and verify technical viability of activities in consultation with communities. Review of available feasibility studies and designs to confirm technical and financial soundness. Proven technologies and approaches widely deployed internationally and in Afghanistan under NSP and CCAP. Project based on 14 years of successful NSP delivery.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Project involves many parties, especially international and local NGOs with limited experience implementing Bank projects. Critical need for coordination among parties on implementation, data collection, and M&E. Risk of weak coordination between central PIU and PIEs. Limited capacity of implementing entities to manage environmental and social risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: AKF and UNOPS leverage existing institutional arrangements with partners. Works carried out by external contractors hired and supervised by AKF and PIEs. Planning and execution in close coordination with communities and Mirabs. UNOPS works through private sector for procurement. Prior approval of staffing and individual procurement staff. Development of Project Operations Manual providing overall control framework. Engagement of ARTF Monitoring Agent. Training of staff on implementation arrangements and project design.

identified_risks.risk_description: Residual fiduciary risks assessed as Substantial. Risks include: absence of tailored Management Information System (MIS) to record and track sub-project expenditures; lack of monitoring by independent third-party monitoring agent; potential non-compliance with financial management requirements by PIEs; liquidity risks in banking system; Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT) risks; fraud and corruption risks; lack of mechanisms to charge staff costs to project; risks that PESCs may not observe applicable controls; assurance of 20% in-kind PESC contributions not properly recorded.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of PIU by both AKF-USA and UNOPS within two months of effectiveness. Prior approval of staffing lists and salary cost charging mechanisms. Development of Project Operations Manual within 6-8 months. Modification of MIS to record, track and report sub-project expenditures. Engagement of ARTF Monitoring Agent for financial and physical monitoring. Due diligence of PIEs with fiduciary assessment by Bank. Subsidiary agreements with standard anti-corruption provisions. Staff required to sign declaration complying with World Bank Anti-Corruption Guidelines. Detailed funds flow arrangements in POM. AML/CFT measures in POM. Hiring of sufficient qualified national procurement staff. Bank provides HEIS for Components 1 and 3. Annual audited financial statements and management letters submitted within six months of year-end.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial E&S risks related to nature of project activities, FCV context, and capacity of AKF and PIEs to manage such risks. Potential adverse environmental impacts include dust, noise, debris, water pollution, soil erosion, sedimentation, improper site restoration, occupational health and safety issues, and electronic waste from solar installations. Opening new wells without proper water quality assessment could lead to arsenic poisoning. Social risks include exclusion of vulnerable groups, management of local expectations around jobs, risk of resistance from ITA on women engagement, SEA/SH risks, non-compliance with labor standards, forced labor risks in solar supply chain, and threats to human security.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preliminary Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), Environmental and Social Commitment Plans (ESCPs), and Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared and disclosed. ESMF includes Labor Management Procedures (LMP), Pest Management Plan (PMP), Integrated Pest Management (IPM) approach, and Voluntary Land Donation Guidelines. Water quality survey before well drilling; testing to ensure quality meets standards. Cascading strategy of ESF requirements through implementing partners. Dedicated E&S staff in both PIUs (environmental specialist, social development specialist, GRM Officer, Gender and SEAH Specialist, security risk management specialist). Stand-alone SEA/SH action plan developed in consultation with stakeholders. Project staff trained on Code of Conduct and SEA/SH implementation. Dedicated GRCs for handling GBV, SEA, SH cases using survivor-centered approach. Non-disclosable security risk management plan based on UNSMS. Forced Labor Performance Declaration and Forced Labor Declaration required from PESCs. Enhanced forced labor language in procurement contracts. Women engaged in decision-making on well placement through CDCs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk associated with varied levels of access to information among population to ensure their engagement in project activities. Risk of exclusion of vulnerable groups from accessing safe water and sanitation facilities.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Extensive communication and awareness campaigns to population. Beneficiary assessment to identify geographical distribution and number of beneficiaries based on inclusion of women in CDCs, socio-demographic characteristics, accessibility of water sources, water quality and health impacts, sanitation access, and waterborne disease incidence. Engagement of women through CDCs (at least 40% of CDC members are female) in decision-making on water well placement. Coordination with Community Resilience and Livelihoods Project (CRLP) to work in communities where women are not prohibited from participating. Prioritization of women-owned and women-employing PESCs. Engagement of female-headed farming households through participatory approaches.

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall procurement risk assessed as High. Key risks include: lack of knowledge and experience of AKF-USA staff with World Bank Procurement Regulations; implementation delays due to supply limits in local market and goods/service delivery constraints; supply and services impacted by post-conflict situation; lack of efficient contract monitoring and management skills; restriction on local banks' operations; risk that UNOPS will not have sufficient procurement staff for new scope of work.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Frequent reporting and regular direct contact between World Bank and AKF-USA/UNOPS. Bank reviews and approves procurement plan and updates via STEP. Close collaboration on technical review of TORs and specifications. Quarterly reports on procurement plan implementation. AKF-USA and PIEs hire sufficient qualified national procurement staff with Bank-financed project experience. ARTF Monitoring Agent provides independent assurance on control framework compliance. Bank provides HEIS for Components 1 and 3. UNOPS hires national and international procurement experts. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents for open international procurement and agreed Sample Procurement Documents for national open competition. Forced labor bidder declarations required for solar procurement.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risks related to lack of official counterpart and discriminating bans of ITA challenging ability of UN and INGOs to work in Afghanistan. If situation changes, local implementing partners may face challenges following business continuity plan.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Clear mechanisms to identify security threats to project and communicate changes in threat levels to parties involved. Business continuity plan for project implementation as much as feasible. Project designed to operate outside direct ITA control with only limited, ringfenced, technical level interactions with select line ministry departments. No direct touch points with ITA other than at technical level if needed to benefit communities and achieve project objectives.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) - Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Projects on International Waterways (OP 7.50) - Exception to riparian notification requirement approved
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Two separate three-tier GRM systems implemented: AKF operates GRM for Components 1 and 3 with separate Grievance Redress Committees (GRCs) at national and regional levels; UNOPS operates GRM for Component 2 with separate GRCs at national and regional levels. Both organizations have functional GRMs in place in various projects in Afghanistan and will extend existing systems to target provinces. Dedicated GRCs for handling Gender-Based Violence (GBV), Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (SEA), and Sexual Harassment (SH) cases using survivor-centered approach. Project-level grievance mechanisms available; communities can also submit complaints to World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service).
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus throughout project. Women and girls are primary beneficiaries as they disproportionately bear burden of water collection and lack of safe water access. Project aims to narrow gender gaps in water sector and meaningfully engage women in implementation. Key gender interventions include: (1) Women engaged in decision-making on water well placement through CDCs (at least 40% of CDC members are female); (2) Provision of safe drinking water and WASH improvements to schools and health facilities with gender-segregated latrines where required; (3) Awareness-raising campaigns on water management and hygiene focusing on women and female students; (4) Coordination with female community health workers to deliver training; (5) Prioritization of women-owned and women-employing PESCs; (6) Engagement of female-headed farming households through participatory approaches; (7) At least 50% female trainees in training programs; (8) Target of 50% female beneficiaries for safe drinking water access (613,440 of 1,226,880); (9) Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) ensuring women's involvement in CDCs is not prohibited; (10) Hiring of both male and female community mobilizers/WASH facilitators; (11) Contribution to UN Basic Human Needs Indicators on female beneficiaries and women-led organizations' consultation. Gender indicators tracked in results framework.
vulnerable_groups: Women and girls (primary collectors of water, exposed to SEA/SH risks, experience mobility restrictions)
Female-headed households (disadvantaged in access to irrigated land and agriculture inputs)
Children under five (diarrheal diseases second most common cause of death)
School-age girls (inadequate WASH in schools barrier to attendance)
Pregnant women and mothers (poor water conditions associated with higher maternal mortality)
Persons with disabilities (implied through vulnerability-based selection criteria)
Poorest households (unable to afford basic health and hygiene supplies)
Communities in drought-affected provinces (16 provinces with severe water scarcity)
Rural populations (66% access to basic drinking water vs 75% nationally; only 6% have piped water)
Survivors of gender-based violence (dedicated GRCs and SEA/SH action plan)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Conflict sensitivity measures include: (1) Working with politically neutral implementing partners (AKF and UNOPS) with long-term presence and community trust; (2) Operating outside direct Interim Taliban Administration (ITA) control through community-based mechanisms (CDCs); (3) Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) ensuring CDCs are not prohibited from operating and women's involvement is not prohibited - if not met, project moves to other areas; (4) Remote project supervision arrangements to mitigate potential ITA interference; (5) Limited, ringfenced, technical-level interactions with ITA departments only when necessary to benefit communities; (6) Leveraging traditional community-led institutions (CDCs, Mirabs, irrigation associations) which remain robust despite political changes; (7) Security risk management plan to identify threats and communicate threat level changes; (8) Business continuity planning for implementation if situation changes; (9) Flexible project design allowing adaptation to changing context; (10) Focus on essential basic services (water and irrigation) that transcend political divisions; (11) Community-driven development approach promoting local ownership and reducing need for government engagement.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces multiple fragility factors: (1) Political instability and regime change (August 2021 Taliban takeover); (2) Cessation of most international aid and all international security assistance; (3) Major economic crisis with 46% of population acutely food-insecure and 6 million on brink of starvation; (4) Poverty at 47% (2019-2020); (5) Severe drought (June 2021) affecting 80% of country with 53% of water points dried up; (6) Climate change making water situation worse (Afghanistan ranked 6th globally on climate vulnerability); (7) Weak and fragmented water sector institutions with slow implementation of reforms; (8) Decades of underinvestment in water infrastructure; (9) Disruption to core services and massive contraction in aggregate demand; (10) Electricity shortages in cities; (11) Only 38% of Afghans have access to grid electricity (11% in rural areas); (12) Ongoing humanitarian crisis with 28 million Afghans requiring humanitarian assistance; (13) Waterborne disease outbreaks (141,361 AWD cases May-August 2022); (14) High maternal mortality (638 per 100,000 live births); (15) Restrictions on women's movements and participation by ITA; (16) Threats to human security through escalation of personal, communal conflict, crime, and violence.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project operates in high-security risk environment. Security considerations include: (1) Non-disclosable security risk management plan based on UN Security Management System (UNSMS) and good international industry practice; (2) Clear mechanisms to identify security threats to project and communicate threat level changes to parties involved; (3) Remote project supervision arrangements by World Bank; (4) Threats to human security through escalation of personal, communal conflict, crime, or violence affecting safety of workers and beneficiaries; (5) Risk of SEA/SH of girls and women when traveling long distances to collect water, exacerbated by ITA mobility restrictions; (6) In Badghis province, 38% of female household members reported feeling unsafe accessing water; (7) Cascading security risk management measures to implementing partners to ensure consistency with ESS1 and ESS4; (8) Project staff trained on security protocols; (9) Business continuity planning for implementation if security situation deteriorates; (10) Flexible project design allowing for adaptation to security changes.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No specific displacement or refugee components mentioned in project design. However, project context includes: (1) Drought-affected populations in 16 provinces requiring emergency water and irrigation support; (2) Vulnerable populations including women, girls, and poorest households prioritized for assistance; (3) Project designed to reduce need for future humanitarian assistance by improving water security and agricultural productivity; (4) Focus on building resilience of rural communities to droughts and other shocks; (5) Beneficiary assessment will identify socio-demographic characteristics of households and accessibility of water sources; (6) Project does not involve land acquisition but uses voluntarily donated land (Voluntary Land Donation Guidelines included in ESMF); (7) No involuntary resettlement anticipated as project focuses on rehabilitation and new construction of water systems in existing communities.
